speaker: Speaker 1

speaker: Speaker 1
text: from the BBC, where we chat every week to some of the biggest leaders in business and finance, shaping how we live and work.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And this week, my colleague Sean Farrington has been swiping right, swiping left.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: No, wrong app, Shawn, but you've been delving into the world of online dating with the boss of one of the biggest players in that particular area.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Yes, I've been speaking to the chief exec of Hinge, one of the biggest dating apps here in the UK around the world as well.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It's held up its sort of user base pretty strongly compared to some of the other dating apps in recent years.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And their story at Hinge has been one as we hear in this interview about focusing on Gen Z in particular.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So we heard a lot about how, how users' habits are changing, users' aspirations are changing, and of course, in a world where technology is changing very, very quickly, how does that play into how we use these apps and how those apps aid, assist us, whatever the word you want to use for that, because Hinge, as you'll hear, and Jackie Jantos, the chief executive there, talked a lot about getting people to go on a date, and we had an interesting discussion about to what extent should AI be able to be used in conversation, in profile building,

speaker: Speaker 2
text: and of course, in a world where technology is changing very, very quickly, how does that play into how we use these apps and how those apps aid, assist us, whatever the word you want to use for that, because Hinge, as you'll hear, and Jackie Jantos, the chief executive there, talked a lot about getting people to go on a date, and we had an interesting discussion about to what extent should AI be able to be used in conversation, in profile building, that that authenticity, that question around that as well.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: She had a lot of interesting things to say about what the struggles for many in Gen Z might be having in life at the moment, whether it's cost of living, loneliness, and how that plays into how people view dating and how they go about that as well.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: The world of events around us, some people trying to push back on, aren't they, against technology and thinking about, you know, joining that running club as a way to perhaps meet more people, meet your next date.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And how that is playing into the thinking at Hinge as well.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So there's plenty to get into with Jackie Jantos, chief executive of Hinge.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Welcome, Jackie Jantos, chief executive of Hinge to the Big Boss interview.

text: Thank you, it's so wonderful to be here.

text: If you don't want to be out of date, as I clearly have been for years.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Um, well,

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Jackie, your insights into different demographics, how they behave,

speaker: Speaker 2
text: how they're behaving right now, you know, it's one of the reasons we've got you on Big Boss interview because um dating apps has been, you know, a phenomenon of recent decades.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And when I was just thinking about like where they sit now in in the world where we are now, whether it's people's aspirations, the advance of technology,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Right.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: love, cost of living as well and how that plays into it all.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Um it's interesting how the the dating app itself can encapsulate quite a bit of that.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Absolutely.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I mean, we are building Hinge for a Gen Z audience.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: this audience is experiencing so much transformation right now. They're also living in a world where unemployment for them is at its high, their lives are being transformed through AI and new technologies and, you know, this is a group that has been spending less and less time in person with others and that's fundamentally changing how they interact with other people.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: and their comfort level of showing up in search of a relationship, which we know is also something that they deeply desire.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So there is a lot going on and sort of a lot to talk about.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Which one of those things do you think he's having the biggest impact on how Hinge works?

speaker: Speaker 2
text: How the dating app scene works right now?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I would say, you know, we know that Gen Z are spending 1,000 less hours in person with each other per year than their same age group two decades ago. So if you just break that down to the simplicity of two plus hours per day, spent not in the company of another human, but most likely sort of going deep in some sort of experience engaged in your phone.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: this sort of prevents people from having the experience of being around others and that is quite you know, a lonely experience.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so we are seeing that this generation disproportionately is feeling lonely and isolated and in the UK, as one example, we know that almost half of Gen Z individuals in the UK are feeling lonely often or always.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: That is the audience who also want relationships, who are coming into Hinge looking for them.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Do you do that?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: They do, we do know that they are also seeking love, but you know, they experience the pandemic during those years of late teens, early twenties, those years when you're sort of experimenting with how you show up in person with another person, how you flirt, how you think about intimacy, you know, so they absolutely want love.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: They're struggling to find ways to find it and struggling to have the confidence to put themselves out there.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So how does that impact how that generation are interacting with Hinge, the fact that they're not so inclined to spend physical time with another human being as as previous generations?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I mean, it impacts everything.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I think, you know, Hinge is an app that we say is designed to be deleted and is that that is not just a marketing line, that's actually how we build our product.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So from the very beginning of the onboarding experience all the way through, we are most motivated to help young users build momentum to get off the app and meet up in person and great dates is what we measure.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So that's that's

speaker: Speaker 2
text: that's the pitch though, but if we know that generation are not spending as much time with other humans, then the conclusion of that would be that that means it's it's harder for the likes of Hinge to actually get people to do that.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yeah.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It's very hard.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, Hinge is a bit of an anomaly in the dating category right now that hasn't been growing, Hinge by contrast has been growing, double digit user growth and revenue year over year.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And that's partly because if you're actually building product to meet the needs of this generation that as I described and helping them get off the app onto a great date, you know, we measure that by asking, have you gone on a date?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And if someone says yes, we say, do you want to go on a second date? And if they say yes to that, that's sort of what triggers a great date.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So if we're orienting our product experience to that, someone who meets their partner on Hinge will tell sort of everyone where they met their partner and that organically drives growth of users wanting to try Hinge again.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Hinge again.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So Hinge is owned by Match.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yes.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And Match has many of the major players in the dating app sort of scene portfolio that people will be aware of.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: including the likes of Tinder.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Why is it that the other apps have seen such a fall in the number of users? What has been going on?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yeah.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, I think what's different about Hinge is the focus on that user outcome.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And that focus has held true for a long time, which means that we have been really focusing on innovating the product experience as users' needs have shifted over the years in order to achieve that outcome. I think one of the things that you'll see many apps doing is sort of following in the wake, also now beginning to focus on user outcomes, because in a category that's about helping you find a relationship,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: a product that actually does that will be the one that continues to grow.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So I chuck Hinge's success up to the fact that we have been very focused on users, we do an enormous amount of research.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We also think about what's happening in culture so we can see around the corner a bit with every next generation.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Are you seeing more individuals using Hinge than you were a year ago, or or fewer people doing that?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We are seeing positive growth in the UK, yes.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Is that individuals using the app or is that off sales overall?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Of individuals using the app.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Right. You know, the way that we think about user growth is again, if you focus on outcomes, the user growth follows, registrations follow, and revenue follows.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And you know, less than 15% of our monthly active users are paying for subscriptions on um on Hinge.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So really the free product and the free experience is what we focus most of our energy on.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And are you seeing the proportion of men increase within that or the proportion of women?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Hinge is a very balanced gender and we are maintaining the consistency of that balance.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I can't speak to the exact numbers, but they haven't changed uh uh I can't speak to the direct numbers for the UK specifically, but they've really haven't changed over the course of the four and a half years that I've been at Hinge.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: where we have initially made this pivot to focus on Gen Z.

speaker: Speaker 2

speaker: Speaker 2
text: you know, this is is it fair to say you're talking 60, 70% of the users are men?

speaker: Speaker 3

speaker: Speaker 2

speaker: Speaker 2

text: And yeah, and and I I just wonder year on year, has is that gone up or down?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yeah.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Uh that has stayed consistent.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So, you know, we focus on female growth quite specifically, so gender balance um on Hinge, especially because it's an app that's about high intent dating is really important to the community for a variety of reasons.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: But uh, you know, the focus for us is on bringing women into the app, you know, this is a category where men sort of follow women and so for us, so long as we stay that focus, um which is the goal,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: because you know, particularly given we are the only dating app at this scale with that type of gender balance, um it's critical for us to maintain that.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So that's a huge focus of our work.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And as you look across the UK, what demographics do you see getting stronger?

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And which ones are maybe getting weaker? What can you tell us about life and dating online around the UK?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yeah, again, you know, we pretty much exclusively focus on a Gen Z dating audience.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We see, um, you know, we see growth across all audiences, but Gen Z is really our core focus at Hinge.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So is your focus.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Does does that mean older generations moving away from dating up?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: No, it doesn't.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: But the way that I think about it is, you know, I've spent my career sort of at the intersection of global brands and businesses and youth culture.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And dating and relationships, it is a deeply human sort of like culturally led thing.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so, for example, if you look at Gen Z as a demographic, this demographic, particularly in the UK, and in many markets, is increasingly um LGBTQA+ representative year over year.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: decade over decade.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So when we look at actually the umbrella of LGBTQ individuals, the fastest growth is coming with the bisexual young women. These aren't necessarily women who have had a sexual experience, but this is how they're identifying.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So for us, if you're building an app again, going back to focusing on user outcomes and going back to building product features that are delivering against the needs of next generation audiences,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: those are the type of macro cultural insights that you need to pay attention to and be building a product for.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So in order to serve next generations, we're thinking about fluidity.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We're thinking about um how we enable experiences that build individual's confidence along the way.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: knowing that they're feeling isolated alone.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And again, the use of technology and that steep decline of time spent in person, this is another macro force that's not going to change over the years.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So I think the way that we think about building product is we build for those future facing audiences, and as we do that,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: you know, when you think about inclusive design, which is a different conversation, but when you build for those with the most sort of acute needs,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: you end up building a product that is better in service of everyone.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So this is what's keeping the momentum at Hinge, it's staying focused on what the next generation is looking for.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And what the next generation needs in order to have a dating experience that feels more fulfilling.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And this is what keeps our product innovating.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We have a number of incredible new features on their way to the UK that have launched in other places.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: things like signals.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, this is a feature that helps you understand which daters on the app are putting in what we believe to be highly intentional, thoughtful, effortful behaviors.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so you can sort of get a sense for a person who you might be interacting with on the app, are they putting in the work?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, one of the things we hear from women in Gen Z and sort of across cohorts is that there's this feeling that this, there's this disproportionate emotional labor put into the dating app experience, and many experiences in life, if I'm honest.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so what they're looking for are signals that other people are putting in an equal amount of effort.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So signals is a really big product feature that launched in the US a couple of days ago.

text: How do you know that?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It's coming here.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: With signals, how do you know that what looks like what might look like a lot of extra effort isn't artificial intelligence being used to do all of that?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I mean, it's a it's an incredible question, you know, a lot of how we know what we know is we have over 15 million daters on the app and we learn from them.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And trust me, they give us so much feedback.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so a product like signals sort of started in the market in test, um, you know, quite a few tests actually,

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It could still be

speaker: Speaker 3
text: to see if what we believe our signals are effort pair with what users on the app perceive to be as signals of effort.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And then,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And then you know, the proof is in what they tell us and the experience they're having.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: um over time and that's how we improve product, you know, that's the sort of beauty of being in tech.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, whether uh, you know, you you can really sort of learn real time, adapt real time.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Of course, you can test out these incredible AI tools, but ultimately, the people on the app who are your customers, they will tell you and you will see whether you're growing or not growing.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: what sort of experience they're truly having.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So if somebody was using as no doubt people are, everybody's using them in all aspects of life now, are using artificial intelligence to it you know, improve their profile and maybe their responses.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: If the person on the receiving end of of that those signals to to put it in your product's language, if they're happy with that, then then Hinge is happy with that.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Well, the way that we think about AI, uh we use AI, uh we absolutely use AI and you know, the first way we use AI, of course is we are a product that sits upon a recommender.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And that is machine learning at its core, so we've been using AI for many, many years, you know.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Well I just mean in terms of customers actual people using your product, using artificial intelligence.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It's not that you look to spot where people are just using AI to upload certain improvements on their profile.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: No.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We So the way that we use AI is, you know, there are three ways at Hinge.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: First is the recommender that I I just described.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: The second is we have a number of embedded tools and features that users use to express themselves through their profiles.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So profiles on Hinge, you know, we we lose about 20% of folks who come onto the app

speaker: Speaker 3
text: through the onboarding experience because the onboarding experience asks quite a lot from you, especially in this category, and we're fine with losing people on that experience because it's intended to be a community of people looking for an intentional relationship.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So prompts as an example, which Hinge invented, is a sort of an icebreaker question that we ask a number to be filled out.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So an example of an AI tool on Hinge is prompt feedback. This is a tool that you can use should you want to.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It will review your prompt and it will help you understand the quality of your prompt.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And you know, spoiler alert, a high quality prompt is one that has quite a bit of detail and is long because ultimately on Hinge, you're building out a rich profile where we're looking for engagement from another person right off the bat of profile review.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Does that fly with authenticity?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So it's modular.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: No.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Because actually, for example, if I'm filling out a prompt and it says, I love watching movies, that's not a particularly strong prompt because it gives no context really to another person looking at my profile.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: But that is how you would respond.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: No way in, so the prompt would say, the prompt would say,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: it would say, tell me more, uh, what kind of movies do you like? And I would say, oh, my favorite movie is Amelie. What do you love about Amelie?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I love that it's this gorgeous love story between these two people and I love watching movies on, you know, every Saturday I go and see a matinee.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Now, when that is in the profile, now, the prompt feedback will tell me that's a great prompt.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And it's a great prompt because it's specific, it's in my words, and what prompt feedback as an AI tool has done,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: has helped extract from me a little bit more detail, so when someone sees my profile, they might say, oh, I also love French films, like, have you seen this one or this one? You know, it's a way in that gives more context.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: That's how we use AI.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So you're comfortable with that in sort of the profile, uh, detail.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It is.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It is helping someone tell more of who they are in their own words.

text: Would you be happy with that in the messaging as well with if people were replying to somebody else and they just said, I like movies?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Yeah, you know, another um another AI tool we have is convo starters.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I think this is getting a little bit closer to what might be in your head, albeit it's slightly different on the app itself.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So convo starters um offers some ways into a conversation, but they're just thought starter ideas.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Like at the end of the day, our app is oriented towards getting you off the app into a date.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So you are going to show up in person as you are to meet someone who has shown up in person as they are.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And if that connection does not feel authentic to who you've been interacting with on the app, Hinge has failed.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And so that is not sort of the space that we play in.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Like we are looking for authenticity, this is a community of people that are highly intentional looking for a real relationship and struggling to find it.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So the work that we do is in service of helping them better express themselves.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Again, going back to a group of people spending less time in person with others.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: There can be a little bit of a lack of confidence.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: a little bit of questioning around how to express yourself and what might be the right things to share.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Is that interesting?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: or how do I lean into a conversation

text: beyond saying like, hey, hey, you know, this is not the type of surface level interaction we're looking for between humans on the app.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: If you're getting to a point where both sides of a conversation are being helped along to have the conversation,

speaker: Speaker 2
text: that you actually you know that you're using this tool, you know the other person is using this tool.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: That that actually

text: you may not you may trust less that you are getting an authentic idea of of what might be possible on a date because of the use of technology so much.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: because of the way technology is used in our app. Um, you know, it's very,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: uh, first you sort of opt into the tools, should you need a little bit of support.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And second, you know, as I described, really they're about helping you express who you are.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: They're not about writing words for you or um having a conversation on your behalf.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: That's really the work that you're you're there to do.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, and quite a lot of the work that we also do because we sit on so much insight from this audience and they tell us so much and we do so much deep research, both of our own users, also of just the general audience, also at like a very high cultural altitude to better understand what's going on and how we might complement and help people on their journey.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We also use all of our marketing channels and tools to really publish research reports on digital body language or you know, what's going on in the LGBTQ+ community in dating.

speaker: Speaker 3

speaker: Speaker 3

speaker: Speaker 3

text: We don't see as much of that. Uh we don't really see that on Hinge because the app is so high intent.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, I talked a little bit earlier about the use of AI and the recommender and some of these tools that help you tell more about yourself.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: The third way we use AI is really our trust and safety tooling.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And AI is a huge unlock.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Explain that a little bit more.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: How you do that?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Well, one example is, you know, large language models, when you think about, uh, say if you're sending someone a message, and our tooling identifies that you may be using a word that could be harmful or could be offensive to someone, you may not even know.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: A lot of people who um send messages, you know, it's um it's a cultural context.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It might not even be intended to be that way.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: We have a tool called, you know, are you sure? And that will pop up and say, are you sure you want to send this message?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And a number of people look at that message, look at the message they just wrote and think, oh, okay, maybe maybe it's this word.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Maybe I should rephrase that and then change and send their message.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: On the flip side, uh AI helps us understand messages that someone's receiving and if something could be harmful could pop up to the recipient and say, did this bother you?

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And then you could give that feedback to us, so the feedback we receive in an instance like that, um, you know, grounded by AI,

speaker: Speaker 3
text: is helping us better understand how we can do a more interventions in that way to make sure that people are respectful towards each other in moments where actually they might not be trying to be disrespectful by design.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: It might just be sort of a cultural nuance.

speaker: Speaker 3

speaker: Speaker 2
text: And something that happens in the world of AI perhaps before people get to dating apps.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: We're hearing increasingly about, you know, not necessarily on a mass scale, but one of the aspects of artificial intelligence and chatbots, the availability of them is is people building relationships with them directly.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, there's uh first I will say that the world, more generally, is scratching the surface of how we use AI.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, for example, Hinge uses Anthropic, Claude comes out with updates every other week.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So we are in sort of the the early journey here.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: I will say there's a couple things that we're seeing, you know, on one hand, uh I was in the UK uh a few months ago uh as well and one of the conversations we were having with young Gen Z daters here was around how folks are using Chat GPT or Chatty, as they called it.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And yes, uh someone was saying that they use their Chatty to have conversations um that are deeply vulnerable to them because they don't want to put the burden on their close friends.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, that sort of breaks my heart because again, going back to having discomfort in person with others as a support in building muscle in terms of how to have a deeper relationship.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: You know, uh I I said to this woman, I think you might be doing a disservice to your friends by not um letting them sort of in on something that you're working through that they would probably love to contribute supporting you through.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So this relationship uh with Chatty on a topic that's quite heavy for someone, I think um, you know, in some ways can add value.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Um but it can also um uh be a disservice to the depth of practicing being in person with others and being in relationship with others that we know that this young group is also um looking for.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And on the flip side, you know, I think you look at there is this emerging frustration and sort of anger from young folks around AI.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: This is a group that are sort of graduating university into a world where unemployment uh is at an incredibly high rate and it's uh their lives are unaffordable and they're hearing all these sort of wild narratives about the workforce sort of going away and and and humans being replaced.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: And this is uh a big sort of like exaggeration that now you see companies as they prepare for IPO, sort of like trying to walk back a little bit.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: So there's um, you know, there's so many themes within the space of AI to pick apart.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Um and I I I
